story:
Dad gave me a telescope for my eighteenth birthday and swore it had a “special feature” I’d have to discover myself. Typical Dad—mysterious, half-proud, half-daring—before he drove off to his part-time gig in what he called NASA’s Gizmo Department. That same night, with Mom at her sister’s in downtown Houston and the house and balcony blissfully mine, I pointed my new toy at Mars, bright as a ruby, second only to Venus. No streetlights out here. The sky felt close enough to touch.

I dialed in the settings—a zoom like a camcorder, extra-boost magnification, fluorite refractor, fast f/ratio, camera adaptor—the works. I zeroed in on Olympus Mons, the Mother of All Volcanoes: sixteen miles tall, older than history, always a favorite landmark. And then my breath froze. The apex blurred, smudged by black smoke that swelled like a bruise. I pulled back, then in again. Lava—or whatever Martian magma is—poured and spread with a freight train’s purpose. Real, not Hollywood. Not a trick. The kind of thing that makes your bones go cold even under a Texas night.

I sprinted inside and flipped on CNN, ready for breaking news to steamroll the President’s goodwill trip to Mexico. Nothing. I surfed channels. Still nothing. I called Dad, voice cracking. “Yo, Dad! Are you guys at NASA watching this? Olympus Mons is alive and belching!” Voicemail. I ran back to the eyepiece.

That’s when I saw them.

At the edge of the rolling fire, fuzzy gray shapes broke and scattered, a sudden burst of motion like ash come alive. The slow ones vanished under the advancing glow; the fast ones fanned out and were gone. Then the smoke swallowed it all. The view went to black wherever I swung the lens. No proof. Only the stampede replaying in my head, as impossible as it was clear. No life on Mars? Tell that to God. Or to whatever was running for its life up there.

I wondered if Dad knew he’d built something that could do this—if anyone else on Earth could see what I’d seen. Or had he decided it was one more not-quite invention and passed it to me like a consolation prize? The telescope’s camera mount, of course, refused to take photos. Perfect.

A car screeched outside. The front door slammed. Dad, all lab coat and momentum, barreled onto the balcony and aimed the scope at Mars.

“Dad, I saw something.”

“Yeah, we saw it too,” he said, eyes to the glass.

“Saw what?” Say it. Say Martians.

“The volcano, of course.” He squinted. “Damn. The whole region’s blacked out.”

I pushed. “Did you spot them?”

He frowned. “Spot what?”

I said it plain. “The Martians.”

He stared like I’d just announced I was moving out. “The hell are you talking about?”

“I saw them! A whole stampede!”

He let it hang, then shut it down. “Go to bed.”

“It works, Dad. Your special zoom—”

“Go to bed, David.” He cut past me, then turned, irritated-teacher voice on. “When magnification gets too high, subjects get dim and lose contrast. What you saw was a spherical aberration. Pincushion distortion. Geometry. Not life.” And he was gone, door slam and all.

Back to CNN: bombings, shootings, social distancing, weird weather. Earth doing Earth. No Mars. No proof. Just me and an afterimage of gray figures fleeing an ocean of fire. Maybe Dad was right about the optics; at that distance, seeing creatures shouldn’t be possible. Or maybe, for once, his sleep-starved tinkering had cracked open a window nobody else knew existed. Either way, exams were tomorrow. I crawled into bed, wide awake, arguing with his defeat and clutching my private miracle.
